note_en:
Stephen Brennan • 22 September 2020 Not that long ago, I found myself wanting to understand gzip. I didn’t necessarily want to learn to implement the algorithm, but rather I just wanted to understand how it was performing on a particular file. Even more specifically, I wanted to understand which parts of a file compressed well, and which ones did not.
 There may be readily available tools for visualizing this, but I didn’t find anything. Since I know gzip is implemented in the Python standard libraries, and I’m familiar with Python plotting libraries, I thought I would try to make my own visualization. This blog post (which is in fact just a Jupyter notebook) is the result.
  Sometimes the hardest part of a data analysis problem is just figuring out what you want to measure. The data is all there, and you have a computer at your disposal, so the possibilities are endless. Knowing  what to compute is tricky. In my case, I want to understand which parts of a file compress well. So it makes sense that whatever I visualize should include the position in the file along the X axis, and the compressed size along the Y axis. An uncompressed file would simply be a diagonal line. The better the compression, the more this line would stay  under the diagonal line of an uncompressed file.
  Since Python supports gzip in the standard library, let’s see how we can measure these X and Y coordinates. First, let’s create a file with some compressed data. My favorite to use in this instance is  Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland, downloaded from Project Gutenberg. We’ll compress it on the command line for simplicity.
 (Note that code prefixed by ‘!’ is executed via bash - everything else is executed in Python).
  -rw-r--r-- 1 stephen stephen 171K Sep 22 20:25 alice.txt-rw-r--r-- 1 stephen stephen 60K Sep 22 20:25 alice.txt.gz
 gzip does a pretty decent job at compressing this, far better than I could do myself. Now, let’s use Python to decompress just a little bit of it, and how much of the original file is consumed as we go.
  In the above code, we save the open file object as  compressed before giving it over to the  GzipFile. That way, as we read the decompressed data out of  gzip_file, we’ll be able to use the  tell() method to see how far we are through the compressed file.
     This feels disappointing. We only read 100 bytes and yet it took 8212 bytes of gzip to give us that data? Well, we have to consider that compression algorithms need to store some tables of data which help decompress the rest of the file, so we should cut gzip some slack. Let’s do this a few more times.
  b' is for the use of anyone anywhere at no cost and with\r\nalmost no restrictions whatsoever. You may '
   This feels wrong. After reading 8212 bytes of compressed data for the first 100 bytes, it takes zero bytes to get the next 100?
  b'copy it, give it away or\r\nre-use it under the terms of the Project Gutenberg License included\r\nwith '
   Clearly there is some buffering going on here. 8212 is suspiciously close to 8192 (20 bytes away) which is a power of two, and thus likely to be a common buffer size. Python’s file I/O machinery is responsible for the buffering, but we can actually get rid of it by disabling buffering.
   Hm. We made  compressed an unbuffered file, but maybe  GzipFile has its own internal buffering. To avoid this, let’s do a bad thing. We can actually set the buffer size for all I/O operations by modifying  io.DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE. If we set it to a small value, then we can reduce the impact of buffering on our measurements. Just for fun, let’s try setting it to 1.
   This seems  much more believable. To read 100 bytes of decompressed data, gzip had to read 205 bytes of compressed data (again, this is probably due to tables and other header information). Let’s continue for a bit:
 bytes_unc  =  100 for  _  in  range ( 5 ):  gzip_file . read ( 100 )  bytes_unc  +=  100  bytes_cmp  =  compressed . tell ()  print ( f 'uncompressed: {bytes_unc} / compressed: {bytes_cmp}' )
 uncompressed: 200 / compressed: 279uncompressed: 300 / compressed: 335uncompressed: 400 / compressed: 376uncompressed: 500 / compressed: 449uncompressed: 600 / compressed: 541
 We can see that after reading 400 uncompressed bytes, the gzip compression has caught up! 376 compressed bytes needed to be read to give us those 400. The gap continues to widen as we go on.
 Now that we’re confident that this approach is giving us interesting data, let’s make some functions to get all of this data for a particular file, so we can visualize it!
  That was just some cleanup. Since modifying the buffer size would likely impact other code we run, it’s best to only modify the buffer size when we need it, and reset it back to its original value when we’re done. This can be done with a context manager.
 import  contextlib @contextlib.contextmanager def  buffer_size ( newsize = 1 ):  old_buffer_size  =  io . DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE  io . DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE  =  newsize  try :  yield  finally :  io . DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE  =  old_buffer_size  with  buffer_size ():  print ( f 'size: {io.DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE}' ) print ( f 'size: {io.DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE}' )
  Now for a function to retrieve compressed and uncompressed sizes. We can do this with a “chunk size” as a parameter. The larger our chunk size, the fewer data points we will have, but the code will run faster. We used 100 as a chunk size above, which seems good enough, but I do prefer a good  round number, so I’ll change it to 64.
 import  pandas  as  pd def  create_compression_curve ( filename ,  chunksize = 64 ):  with  buffer_size ( 1 ),  open ( filename ,  'rb' )  as  fileobj :  gf  =  gzip . GzipFile ( fileobj = fileobj )  records  =  []  read  =  0  while  True :  data  =  gf . read ( chunksize )  if  len ( data )  ==  0 :  break  # end of file  else :  read  +=  len ( data )  records . append (( read ,  fileobj . tell ()))  df  =  pd . DataFrame ( records ,  columns = [ 'uncompressed' ,  filename ])  return  df . set_index ( 'uncompressed' ) ccurve  =  create_compression_curve ( 'alice.txt.gz' ) ccurve
  The above function simply reads the gzipped file in chunks, measuring the distance we’ve gone through the compressed file each time, and adding it to a list of “records”. This list is converted into a Pandas Dataframe, which is commonly used to hold tabular data like this. We set the “uncompressed” column to be the “index”, since that’s what we’d consider the X-axis.
 The result looks exciting! We can even go right ahead and plot it from here.
 # Some style changes to make the plots more pretty import  matplotlib.pyplot  as  plt import  matplotlib  as  mpl plt . style . use ( 'ggplot' ) mpl . rcParams [ 'figure.figsize' ]  =  [ 16 ,  8 ] ccurve . plot ()
  Well, I gotta give it to gzip – it’s pretty consistent. I can’t really see anything interesting in the plot, except that the gzipped data is smaller than the uncompressed version (duh). We can add this in to make it more explicit:
    So, the result here seems to be blindingly mundane. gzip compresses reasonably well, it’s obviously better than uncompressed.
 Well, let’s try to make things less mundane. First, a peek at the gzip(1) manual page indicates that it has different compression levels 1-9. I’ll let the manual do the explaining:
 -# --fast --best Regulate the speed of compression using the specified digit #, where -1 or --fast indicates the fastest compression method (less compression) and -9 or --best indicates the slowest compression method (best com‐ pression). The default compression level is -6 (that is, biased to‐ wards high compression at expense of speed).
  import  os files  =  [] for  level  in  range ( 1 ,  10 ):  os . system ( f 'gzip -k -S .gz.{level} -{level} alice.txt' )  files . append ( f 'alice.txt.gz.{level}' )  print ( f 'Created {files[-1]}' )
  Above I went ahead and created all the different compression levels. Now, we can get compression curves for all of them and plot them:
    That seems slightly more interesting. The default compression level of 6 seems to be chosen well. Beyond level 6, the reduction in file size seems pretty difficult to notice. However, the difference between the compression ratios is rather small compared to the uncompressed line:
     Ok, so we just saw that gzip’s compression levels  do in fact work as the manual page describes. But at the end of the day everything just looks like a line. And this isn’t really that surprising. Human language is a textbook example of data which is easy to compress, and it’s pretty consistent. What if we created a file which wasn’t like that?
 We could combine random data (difficult to compress) with human language to create regions of a file which compress at different ratios, and see what we get!
 Below I’ll create a file using a series of  dd commands, compress it, and plot it.
 ! dd  if = alice . txt  of = special . data  bs = 4096  count = 10 ! dd  if =/ dev / urandom  of = special . data  bs = 4096  count = 10  oflag = append  conv = notrunc ! dd  if = alice . txt  of = special . data  bs = 4096  count = 10  oflag = append  conv = notrunc  skip = 10 ! dd  if =/ dev / urandom  of = special . data  bs = 4096  count = 10  oflag = append  conv = notrunc ! dd  if = alice . txt  of = special . data  bs = 4096  count = 10  oflag = append  conv = notrunc  skip = 20 ! dd  if =/ dev / urandom  of = special . data  bs = 4096  count = 10  oflag = append  conv = notrunc ! dd  if = alice . txt  of = special . data  bs = 4096  count = 10  oflag = append  conv = notrunc  skip = 30 ! dd  if =/ dev / urandom  of = special . data  bs = 4096  count = 10  oflag = append  conv = notrunc ! gzip  - k  special . data
 10+0 records in10+0 records out40960 bytes (41 kB, 40 KiB) copied, 0.000172505 s, 237 MB/s10+0 records in10+0 records out40960 bytes (41 kB, 40 KiB) copied, 0.000732935 s, 55.9 MB/s10+0 records in10+0 records out40960 bytes (41 kB, 40 KiB) copied, 0.000242876 s, 169 MB/s10+0 records in10+0 records out40960 bytes (41 kB, 40 KiB) copied, 0.000810064 s, 50.6 MB/s10+0 records in10+0 records out40960 bytes (41 kB, 40 KiB) copied, 0.000156087 s, 262 MB/s10+0 records in10+0 records out40960 bytes (41 kB, 40 KiB) copied, 0.000727009 s, 56.3 MB/s10+0 records in10+0 records out40960 bytes (41 kB, 40 KiB) copied, 0.000379906 s, 108 MB/s10+0 records in10+0 records out40960 bytes (41 kB, 40 KiB) copied, 0.00074872 s, 54.7 MB/s
   Here’s some fun data! I know the above plot looks like it only has a few data points, but it actually has one point every 64 bytes along the X axis. The piecewise nature of the plot is just due to how obviously different gzip performs on the two different types of data. The first segment is text data, and so the slope is not very steep. The second segment is random data, which does not compress well, and so the slope is steep. This alternates for all the segments.
  An initramfs is a small filesystem which gets loaded just after your OS boots. It contains drivers and configuration data necessary to get your computer to the point where it can mount the  real filesystem in all its glory. It also happens to be (on my machine) gzip compressed.
    Who knows. I didn’t really have a purpose in doing this, beyond curiosity. And as you can see, not much came from it beyond some plots of squigly lines.
 There are some other compression algorithms implemented in the Python standard library. I could try to compare compression algorithms on the same file, which could be interesting.
 I could also try to find more exciting compressed files. There must be something out there which creates a cool compression curve!
 In any case, I’d encourage you, the reader, to hack on this code and find something interesting for yourself.

彭博社是否试图收买佛罗里达州的选票？一位亿万富翁为3.1万名重罪犯还清了2000万美元的债务，这样他们就可以在该州投票，该州只有537票决定了2000年的总统选举。
彭博社为佛罗里达州的3.1万名重罪犯还清了2000万美元的债务，这样他们就可以投票了。
就在几天前，佛罗里达州州长罗恩·德桑蒂斯(Ron DeSantis)赢得了一场法庭胜利，阻止重刑犯在支付罚款、赔偿和法庭费用之前投票。
其他捐赠者包括约翰·传奇(John Legend)、勒布朗·詹姆斯(LeBron James)、迈克尔·乔丹(Michael Jordan)、MTV、喜剧中心、VH1、Ben&amp；Jerry‘s、Levi Strauss&amp；Co和迈阿密海豚队(Miami Dolphins。
亿万富翁迈克·布隆伯格(Mike Bloomberg)已经为佛罗里达州3.1万多名重罪犯还清了2000万美元的债务，这样他们就可以在该州投票，该州只有537票决定了2000年的总统选举。
这位前民主党总统候选人介入帮助已经完成刑期的重刑犯，这样他们就可以在11月3日投票。
身家超过500亿美元的布隆伯格筹集了2000多万美元来帮助他的努力，同时也承诺提供1亿美元帮助乔·拜登赢得佛罗里达州。
我们知道，要赢得佛罗里达州，我们需要说服、激励拜登专栏，并为其增加新的选票。彭博社在一份书面声明中表示，这意味着我们需要探索所有途径，在这么多选票已经确定的情况下找到所需的选票。
投票权是我们民主的基础，任何美国人都不应被剥夺这一权利。声明补充说，我们与佛罗里达权利恢复联盟合作，决心结束剥夺公民权和一直推动这一现象的歧视。
彭博社的一位顾问告诉“华盛顿邮报”：迈克想要完成这件事有两个原因。第一，因为这是为了民主而做的正确的事情。第二，因为它会立即激活数以万计的选民，他们倾向于投票给乔·拜登(Joe Biden)。
就在几天前，佛罗里达州州长罗恩·德桑蒂斯(Ron DeSantis)赢得了一场法庭胜利，阻止重刑犯投票，直到他们支付了罚款、赔偿和法庭费用。
9月11日，一家联邦上诉法院裁定，佛罗里达州的重刑犯除了服刑外，还必须支付所有罚款、恢复原状和法律费用，才能重新获得投票权。
此案可能会对11月的选举产生广泛影响。佛罗里达州有29张选举人票，这对唐纳德·特朗普总统入主白宫的希望至关重要。
根据佛罗里达州选民在2018年以压倒性多数通过的修正案4，完成刑期的重刑犯将恢复投票权。共和党议员随后采取行动，定义完成一句话意味着什么。
除了服刑时间外，立法者还指示，所有法律经济义务，包括未付的罚款和恢复原状，也必须在重刑犯有资格投票之前解决。
据彭博社工作人员称，在彭博社呼吁再筹集近1700万美元之前，佛罗里达权利恢复联盟已经筹集了约500万美元。
这笔钱的目标是那些在法律受到质疑时登记投票的重刑犯，他们的欠款不超过1500美元。
工作人员说，这约占3.11万人。在一个以537票决定2000年总统选举的州，在民调显示特朗普和拜登势均力敌的一年里，这可能是至关重要的。
该组织的组织者表示，他们的目标不是在特定政党注册的人。
该组织的执行董事德斯蒙德·米德(Desmond Meade)表示，让政治见鬼去吧，让其他任何暗示或影射见鬼去吧，归根结底，这是关于真实的人、真实的生活，以及想要成为其中一部分的美国公民。
该组织表示，其他捐赠者包括约翰·传奇、勒布朗·詹姆斯、迈克尔·乔丹、音乐电视公司、喜剧中心、VH1、本·杰瑞公司、李维斯·施特劳斯公司、迈阿密海豚乐团、奥兰多魔术、迈阿密热火和斯蒂芬·斯皮尔伯格。
note_en:
Is Bloomberg trying to buy the Florida vote? Billionaire pays off more than $20m in debt for 31,000 felons so they can vote in the state where just 537 votes decided the presidential election in 2000
 Bloomberg paid off $20M in debt for 31,000 felons in Florida so they can vote
 The move comes just days after Florida Gov Ron DeSantis won a court victory to keep felons from voting until they've paid off fines, restitution and court fees
 Other donors include John Legend, LeBron James, Michael Jordan, MTV, Comedy Central, VH1, Ben & Jerry's, Levi Strauss & Co and the Miami Dolphins
        Billionaire Mike Bloomberg has paid off $20million in debt for more than 31,000 felons in  Florida so that they can vote in the state where just 537 votes decided the presidential election in 2000.
 The former Democratic presidential candidate stepped in to help felons who have completed their prison sentences so they can vote on November 3.
 Bloomberg, who is worth more than $50billion, raised more than $20million to assist in his endeavor while also pledging $100million to help  Joe Biden win Florida.
 'We know to win Florida we will need to persuade, motivate and add new votes to the Biden column. This means we need to explore all avenues for finding the needed votes when so many votes are already determined,' Bloomberg said in a written statement.
   'The right to vote is fundamental to our democracy and no American should be denied that right. Working together with the Florida Rights Restoration Coalition, we are determined to end disenfranchisement and the discrimination that has always driven it,' the statement added.
 A Bloomberg adviser told the  Washington Post: 'Mike wanted to get this done for two reasons. One, because it’s the right thing to do for the democracy. And two, because it immediately activates tens of thousands of voters who are predisposed to vote for Joe Biden.'
 The move comes just days after Florida Gov Ron DeSantis won a court victory to keep felons from voting until they've paid off fines, restitution and court fees.
  A federal appellate court ruled on September 11 that in addition to serving their sentences, Florida felons must pay all fines, restitution and legal fees before they can regain their right to vote.
 The case could have broad implications for the November elections. Florida has 29 electoral college votes that are crucial to President Donald Trump's hopes of staying in the White House.
 Under Amendment 4, which Florida voters passed overwhelmingly in 2018, felons who have completed their sentences would have voting rights restored. Republican lawmakers then moved to define what it means to complete a sentence.
   In addition to prison time served, lawmakers directed that all legal financial obligations, including unpaid fines and restitution, would also have to be settled before a felon could be eligible to vote.
 The Florida Rights Restitution Coalition had raised about $5million before Bloomberg made calls to raise almost $17million more, according to Bloomberg staffers.
 The money is targeted for felons who registered to vote while the law was in question and who owe $1,500 or less.
 That accounts for about 31,100 people, the staffers said. In a state that decided the 2000 presidential election by 537 votes, that could be critical in a year when polls show Trump and Biden in a dead heat.
 Organizers for the group say they aren't targeting people registered with a particular political party.
 'To hell with politics, to hell with any other implications or inuations, at the end of the day it's about real people, real lives, American citizens who want to be a part of this,' said Desmond Meade, the group's executive director.
  The group said other donors include John Legend, LeBron James, Michael Jordan, MTV, Comedy Central, VH1, Ben & Jerry's, Levi Strauss & Co, the Miami Dolphins, the Orlando Magic, the Miami Heat and Stephen Spielberg.

note:
这轮融资由Mouro Capital(最近剥离出来的基金，前身为桑坦德创新风险投资公司(Santander InnoVentures)牵头)，现有投资者Global Founders Capital、SeedCamp和White Star Capital参与其中。许多天使投资者也投资了Uncaped，包括TransferWise的Taavet Hinrikus，LendInvest的Christian Faes，Butternut Box的David Nolan&amp；Kevin Glynn和GoCarless的卡洛斯·冈萨雷斯-卡德纳斯。
去年，由“连续创业者”阿什尔·伊斯梅尔(他最近担任Midrive的首席执行官)和前风险投资人皮奥特·皮萨兹创立的Uncaped已经着手利用各种营销、销售和会计数据，以便能够根据年轻企业当前(和预计)的收入为其提供融资。它诞生于两人自己对欧洲企业家可用资金选择有限的不满，即股权融资或传统债务融资。
作为第三种选择，Uncaped提供它所谓的“增长融资”，以换取低至6%的固定费用。企业只在创收时偿还资本，“没有固定的还款日期，也没有复利、股权或个人担保”。
它最初的定位是特别适合希望投资在线营销以更快增长销售额的创收公司，但后来将目标扩大到其他场景。
伊斯梅尔说：“当我们推出的时候，我们知道出售股权购买Facebook和谷歌广告对创始人来说是一笔糟糕的交易。”“因此，这就是我们重点关注的领域，主要是为公司提供广告和库存方面的资金。从那时起，我们开始帮助公司获得资金，用于任何目的，包括推出新产品、扩大国际业务或扩大团队。“。
推出后，通过改进技术，Uncaped将做出贷款决定的时间从3天缩短到了几个小时。一旦融资达成一致，这家初创公司现在也可以发行Visa卡，这样创始人就可以立即开始消费资金。
伊斯梅尔说：“有了这轮新的投资，我们也开始为只有6个月营收历史的早期公司提供资金(以前我们的最低营收历史是9个月)。”“我们还将一次预付款增加了一倍，达到200万英镑。因此，现在更多的公司可以更快地为更多的用例获得资金。“。
当被问及冠状病毒大流行如何影响无上限评估风险的方式时，过去的收入表现在未来可能不是一个可靠的指标，皮萨兹表示，在某些方面，经济衰退是建立信贷模型的最佳时机，“因为其他所有人的模型现在都无关紧要了”。
他告诉我：“其他在线贷款机构依赖个人担保和创始人自己的信用记录作为其风险模型的主要驱动因素，而我们使用有关公司实际交易的实时数据来做出决定。”“当大流行来袭时，我们能够获得有关我们的投资组合表现如何的实时数据，迅速做出决定，并继续发放信贷，而其他人则被迫撤资。自那以后，我们的投资数量和回报都呈指数级增长。“。
皮萨兹表示，Uncaped继续升级其承销技术，现在看到不同的商业模式如何适应更具挑战性的时代，它对自己的承销建立了更多信心。
“与风投不同的是，我们需要整个投资组合取得成功，而不仅仅是几个弥补其他投资组合损失的投资组合。因此，我们在决策过程中需要有很大的确定性，“他承认。
伊斯梅尔补充说：“股权是为数字广告支出和可重复增长提供资金的最昂贵的方式，由于大流行使筹款变得更加困难，它只会变得更加昂贵。”因此，我们的甜蜜点仍然是直接面向消费者的产品和其他快速货币化的初创企业，这些初创企业的月销售额在1万-200万GB之间，拥有健康的单位经济，并希望避免稀释它们的创始人和现有投资者。
“对于仍处于研发阶段或需要更多时间推向市场的企业来说，风险投资仍然是一个很好的解决方案，但对于已经存在、拥有可预测的客户获取成本和可重复增长的企业，我们知道我们是一个更好的选择。”
note_en:
The round was led by  Mouro Capital   (the recently spun-out fund  previously called Santander InnoVentures), with participation from existing investors Global Founders Capital, Seedcamp and White Star Capital. Various angel investors have also backed Uncapped, including Taavet Hinrikus (TransferWise), Christian Faes (LendInvest), David Nolan & Kevin Glynn (Butternut Box) and Carlos Gonzalez-Cadenas (GoCardless).
 Founded last year by “serial entrepreneur” Asher Ismail (who was most recently CEO of Midrive) and former VC Piotr Pisarz, Uncapped has set out to use various marketing, sales and accounting data to be able to offer finance for young businesses based on their current (and projected) revenue. It was born out of the pair’s own frustrations with the limited funding options available to European entrepreneurs, namely equity-based or traditional debt financing.
  Framed as a third option,  Uncapped   provides what it terms “growth finance” in return for a flat fee as low as 6%. Businesses only repay the capital as they generate revenue, “with no set repayment date and no compounding interest, equity or personal guarantees”.
 It was originally pitched as being particularly suited to revenue-generating companies wishing to invest in online marketing to grow sales more quickly, but has since widened its target to other scenarios.
 “When we launched, we knew selling equity to buy Facebook and Google ads was a bad deal for founders,” says Ismail. “So this is where we focused and mainly funded companies for advertising and inventory. Since then we’ve set out to help companies get funding for any purpose including launching a new product, expanding internationally, or growing the team”.
 Post-launch and through refining its technology, Uncapped has sped up the time it takes to make a lending decision from three days to a few hours. Once financing has been agreed, the startup can also now issue Visa cards so founders can start spending funds immediately.
 “With this new investment round, we’ve also started funding earlier-stage companies with only six months of revenue history (previously our minimum was nine months),” says Ismail. “We’ve also doubled the amount we can advance at one time to £2 million. So now more companies can access funding for more use cases, and faster”.
 Asked how the coronavirus pandemic has affected the way Uncapped evaluates risk, where past revenue performance may not be a reliable indicator going forward, Pisarz says a recession in some ways is the best time to build a credit model, “as everyone else’s models are now irrelevant”.
 “Whereas other online lenders depend on personal guarantees and the founder’s own credit history as the main drivers of their risk models, we use live data about the company’s actual trading to make our decisions,” he tells me. “When the pandemic hit, we were able to access the real-time data about how our portfolio was performing, make quick decisions and continue to issue credit, where others were forced to pull back. Our number of investments and the returns have since grown exponentially”.
 To boot, Pisarz says Uncapped has continued to upgrade its underwriting technology, and having now seen how different business models adjust to more challenging times, it has built more confidence in its underwriting.
 “Unlike a VC, we need our whole portfolio to succeed, not just a few that make up for the losses of the others. So we need to have a lot of certainty in our decision making,” he concedes.
 “Equity was the most expensive way to fund digital ad spend and repeatable growth, and as fundraising has become even harder due to the pandemic, it has only become more expensive,” adds Ismail. “So our sweet spot continues to be direct-to-consumer products and other fast-monetising startups that generate between £10,000-£2 million of monthly sales, have healthy unit economics and want to avoid diluting their founders and existing investors.
 “For businesses that are still in the R&D stage or need more time to get to market, venture is still a good solution, but for businesses who are already live, have predictable customer acquisition costs, and repeatable growth, we know we are a better option”.
title: 无上限再获得2600万GB，为欧洲企业家提供基于收入的融资
title_en: Uncapped picks up another £26M to offer revenue-based finance to European entrepreneurs

note:
两家公司都没有披露合伙企业的财务条款，也没有披露少数股权的比例。据一位不愿透露姓名的知情人士透露，Traton确实向TuSimple进行了直接资本投资。目前还不清楚它是否也包括实物捐赠。
Traton的投资并不是TuSimple最近在E系列融资2.5亿美元的努力的一部分。Raton Group确实有权参与这轮融资。
两家公司周三概述了推出一项开发计划的计划，该计划将使用Traton的Scania卡车，该卡车配备了TuSimple的自动化车辆技术。两家公司表示，测试将从Södertälje到瑞典Jönköping的路线开始。这家卡车制造商最终希望在瑞典、德国和其他国家的道路上测试无人驾驶卡车车队。
除了“密切合作”为Traton品牌的卡车开发4级自动驾驶系统的计划外，两家公司没有提供该计划的范围或时间表的细节。Traton表示，具体而言，目标是让这些卡车具备4级能力，他们将其定义为能够“在规定的驾驶条件下实现完全自动化，无需人工干预，并适用于所有市场.”
特拉顿说，这可以作为一种手段，在中期内抵消日益缺乏的司机的影响。Traton在其公告中表示，第一批部署案例可以在特别划定的区域以外进行，特别是对于频繁出行的枢纽到枢纽航线。
这一合作关系将把TuSimple的自动驾驶汽车业务从美国和中国扩展到欧洲。这家初创公司得到了新浪(Sina)、英伟达(Nvidia)、联合包裹(UPS)和一级供应商Mando Corporation的支持，在美国运营着一支由40辆自动驾驶卡车组成的车队，用于测试和运送亚利桑那州和德克萨斯州之间的货物。该公司成立于2015年，在中国、圣地亚哥和亚利桑那州图森都有业务。
此次合作还加强了Traton、TuSimple和Navistar之间的联系。今年7月，TuSimple宣布计划与Navistar合作，在2024年之前开发并开始生产自动半自动卡车。该计划是不再改造TuSimple目前使用的Navistar International商用卡车，而是开发专门为自动驾驶设计的半卡车。战略合作伙伴关系包括Navistar收购初创公司TuSimple的未披露股份。
与此同时，Traton持有Navistar 16.6%的股份，该公司在2016年9月通过购买价值2.56亿美元的股票获得了Navistar。这两家公司最终可能会变得更加亲密。今年1月，Traton主动出价，以35美元的价格收购其不拥有的剩余Navistar股票。彭博社(Bloomberg)本月早些时候报道，Traton本月将出价上调至每股43美元，约合39亿美元。
note_en:
Neither company disclosed the financial terms of the partnership or the percentage of the minority stake. Traton did make a direct capital investment into TuSimple, according to one unnamed source familiar with the deal. It’s unclear if it also included in-kind contributions.
 Traton’s investment is not part of TuSimple’s recent effort to  raise $250 million in a Series E round. raton Group does have an option to participate in the round.
  The two companies outlined Wednesday plans to launch a development program that will use Traton’s Scania trucks equipped with TuSimple’s automated vehicle technology. The companies said testing will begin with a route between Södertälje to Jönköping in Sweden. The trucking manufacturer ultimately wants to test driverless truck fleets on roads throughout Sweden, Germany and other countries.
 The companies didn’t provide details on the scope of the program or timelines, beyond plans to “work closely” to develop Level 4 autonomous systems for trucks of the Traton brands. Specifically, Traton said the aim is for these trucks to have Level 4 capabilities, which they define as being able to “achieve full automation without human intervention under defined driving conditions and applied in all markets.”
 Traton said this could serve as a means of counteracting the increasing lack of drivers in the medium term. The first cases of deployment could be conducted outside of the specially demarcated areas, particularly for highly frequented hub-to-hub routes, Traton said in its announcement.
 The partnership will expand TuSimple’s autonomous vehicle operations beyond the U.S. and China to Europe. The startup, which is backed by Sina, Nvidia, UPS and Tier 1 supplier Mando Corporation, operates a fleet of 40 self-driving trucks in the U.S. that are used for testing and to carry freight between Arizona and Texas. The company, which launched in 2015 and has operations in China, San Diego and Tucson, Arizona.
 The partnership also tightens the connection between Traton, TuSimple and Navistar. In July, TuSimple announced plans to develop and begin producing autonomous semi trucks by 2024 in partnership with Navistar. The plan is to move away from retrofitting the Navistar International commercial trucks that TuSimple currently uses and instead develop semi trucks specifically designed for autonomous operations. The strategic partnership included Navistar taking an undisclosed stake in startup TuSimple.
 Meanwhile, Traton holds a 16.6% stake in Navistar, which it acquired by buying $256 million worth of shares in September 2016. The two companies could end up getting closer. In January, Traton made an unsolicited bid to buy the remaining Navistar stock that it doesn’t own for $35 share. Traton upped its offer this month to $43 a share, or about $3.9 billion,  Bloomberg reported earlier this monthh.
title: 大众Traton集团与TuSimple达成协议开发自动驾驶卡车
title_en: VW’s Traton Group strikes a deal with TuSimple to develop autonomous trucks

note:
多年来，我一直隐藏着一个可耻的秘密：我曾经帮助解开了一个魔方，但这完全是偶然的，我太骄傲了，再也不想完成这个谜题了。
我希望我可以吹嘘说，我通过纯粹的脑力掌握了魔方的谜题。然而，完成这一壮举的9岁版的我当时几乎没有注意到这一点。我和我的朋友凯西(Casey)一起度过了一个周末的下午，他断断续续地换了几分钟各种不同的面孔。当他喊了一句不能重复的脏话时，我低头一看，我几乎解决了这该死的事情。我们一起学习，几分钟就完成了，然后向我们的小学密友吹嘘。他们希望我们重复这项任务。我们永远做不到。
我一直在想魔方的创建者ErnőRubik会怎么想我的经历，我终于有机会问他了。在他的新书“立方：我们所有人的谜题”中，这位匈牙利发明家和建筑师非常详细地解释了对知识的大胆渴求是如何使他的生活充满活力的。当然，他讲述了他是如何发明了以他的名字命名的玩具的故事，以及他的童年是如何帮助他成为今天的他的。然而，更进一步，鲁比克将他的立方体用作知识本身的性质的寓言，这是一个被称为认识论的研究领域。
在这样做的过程中，Rubik做出了许多断言，其中每一个都非常明智。他坚持认为，玩耍，许多成年人认为这是一种幼稚的浪费时间，实际上对健康的智力发展和为社会创造伟大事物的能力都是必不可少的，这一点许多成年人都不屑一顾，认为这是一种幼稚的浪费时间的行为，实际上对健康的智力发展和为社会创造伟大事物的能力都是必不可少的。他认为，在我们的文化中，好奇心是一种被低估的美德，我们应该鼓励人们寻求知识，仅仅因为它有趣和宣泄，而不是以我们找到的信息能带来一些金钱回报为条件。事实上，尽管他显然不喜欢在国家社会主义的经济体系中长大，但鲁比克写道，这确实带来了创造的好处，完全无视经济收益，这意味着创造性的人可以行使他们的智力，以实现他们自己的目的。
不过，或许最能说明问题的是，鲁比克表示，他喜欢魔方是成功和失败的健康缩影这一事实。当然，对他来说，这是一次成功，因为它让他在40岁之前就过上了舒适的小康生活。然而，即使它从未成为商业轰动，Rubik写道，他仍然会认为这是一项成就，原因很简单，他能够发明这样一个成功的拼图。除此之外，Rubik指出，即使是那些未能成功解决他的难题的人，也会从他们的努力中学习。
以下是对Rubik的电子邮件采访的文字记录；和往常一样，这篇采访经过浓缩和编辑，以供印刷。
你的童年是如何为你成年后发展魔方的能力播下种子的？
由于魔方没有跟随任何以前存在的东西，所以很难特别指出任何东西。话虽如此，我从很小的时候就非常喜欢玩脑筋急转弯。我也喜欢单独创作的过程，比如素描和绘画。我父亲对我的影响很大，尽管相比之下，我认为自己是一个有创造力的业余爱好者。我父亲既是设计师又是发明家，是他所在领域的专业人士。在20世纪60年代初，他创造了他最著名的飞机，R-26 Góbé，成为匈牙利经典的训练滑翔机，并出口到古巴、奥地利和英国。我当然意识到，每次他弄清楚设计的结构、材料和所有细节时，他都是在解决非常实用和复杂的难题。
魔方在很大程度上是为了理解我们世界的复杂性。在某些领域，比如群论或组合学，这种联系是非常具体和直接的。在其他领域，它确实更具隐喻性：如何思考问题的解决。当回报只不过是解决方案本身时，人性是什么让我们可以奢侈地花费如此多的时间和精力来解决一个看似不可能的挑战呢？
在多大程度上，当你写这篇文章的时候，你是在用你和魔方的关系来比喻父母对孩子的感觉，或者任何创造者对他们的创造的感觉吗？
当一个人为人父母时，我们的生活发生了实质性的变化。在父亲的余生和以后，孩子成为他或她故事中的中心人物。这种联系是永恒的-但是一个孩子有他们自己的，独立的生活。我非常喜欢魔方，我确实有很深的责任感，但我理解魔方的故事不是我的。他成为了我个人从未梦想过的超级巨星。
魔方是我创造的，但另一个父母一定是大自然母亲。虽然我创造了它，但它也改变了我，它也打开了我的生活。
你在书中承认你讨厌写作，但你还是这样做了，这样你就可以更好地理解我们认为理所当然的事情，什么让人们创造新东西，以及他们是如何受到启发去制造以前从未创造过的东西的，你在书中承认了自己不喜欢写作，但你还是这样做了，这样你就可以更好地理解我们认为理所当然的事情，什么让人们创造新东西，以及他们是如何受到启发去制造以前从未创造过的东西的。在你的书中，你在多大程度上能够回答这些问题？
显然，这些问题没有明确的答案。在这些话题上，我自己的想法变得更加清晰，我希望我能把其中的一些想法传达给立方体的读者。然而，这本书本身，就像“立方体”一样，永远不会真正结束。我们总是可以一次又一次地重新审视这些关于人性的永恒问题，我们也应该这样做。
你在书中提到，立方体是如何将人们凝聚在一起的，这给了你灵感。那么立方体是如何使这成为可能的呢？
就社区建设而言，速立方一直是最重要的发展。立方体爱好者已经逐渐从纯粹的草根努力中创造了一项全球运动。这一切都始于20世纪80年代初，但随着互联网为利基社区提供了寻找彼此的平台，它真正获得了发展势头。今天，每年在100多个国家举办1000多场速度立方体比赛。友谊甚至婚姻的建立都要归功于魔方--但我担心魔方也要为几起离婚事件负责。
更广泛地说，关于好奇心，你认为人们可以从你发明的魔方中学到什么？这似乎是一个适用于智力、艺术和其他专业和创意学科的信息。
我认为好奇心就像口渴或饥饿。这是一种填补空白、抚慰智力和情感之痒的驱动力。带着好奇心，我们感觉到有什么东西在那里，但我们不太确定那是什么。它是能够点燃创造力的火焰。有一种内在的动力需要跟进，还有一种承诺，那就是可能会发现一些隐藏的东西，而我可能是唯一看不见的人。表面之下的是理解的需要。从这个意义上说，我们不是创造者，而是发现者。
拼图带来了我们自己的重要品质，比如好奇心本身。拼图不仅仅是娱乐或消磨时间的装置。对我们来说，就像我们的祖先一样，它们为我们的创造潜力指明了方向。如果你很好奇，你会发现你周围的拼图。如果你下定决心，你会解决它们的。
好奇心意味着我们不认为事情是理所当然的，我们一次又一次地质疑甚至是基本面。我们之所以找到好问题，是因为我们对事物的“如何”感兴趣。这是唯一的前进道路。在这里，前进并不是指预先确定的方向或可以预先确定的东西，而是指不仅是眼睛的开放，而且是更广阔的视野的开放。
你讨论你对教育重要性的信念，同时对我们如何做到这一点提出批评。两个让我印象深刻的问题是，当你指出我们并没有真正让年轻人做好开发他们潜在智力的准备，并以有意义的方式将其应用于外部世界时，你引用的话是，我一直讨厌用像智商测试这样的东西来量化智力的整个想法，并同情那些认为这项测试只衡量他们在智商测试中表现出色的能力的人。你认为我们应该如何改变我们的教育方式？
如今，我们在教育环境中真正能衡量的是孩子们发展技能所需的努力。
作为一种可靠的智力衡量标准，有争议的智商测量仍然具有独特的吸引力，而且我们经常忘记了人类智力的另一个关键方面：情感。理解人类行为、感受和解码周围人的迹象的能力，你需要能够唤醒他们的一些情感共鸣。
魔方是少数几个所谓的教育娱乐是无缝体验的例子之一。老师们很高兴地接受了它作为一种教学工具，学生们享受着魔方可以提供的好玩的学习。
那些希望成为自学成才的人(你鼓励人们渴望成为自学成才的人)如何才能以一种将事实与虚构、知识与虚假知识分开的方式追求自己的兴趣，从从事浪费时间的活动中发展有用的技能呢？
人们不一定希望成为自学成才的人。他们有时只是非常喜欢或好奇某件事，以至于他们花了大量的时间和精力去正确理解它。真正的好奇心总是诚实和开放的--它与偏见和先入为主正好相反。就像拼图总是公平的一样--如果你还在作弊，那你就是在欺骗你自己！
鲁比克的书“立方：我们所有人的谜题”现在正在熨斗图书公司出售。
note_en:
For years I have harbored a shameful secret: I once helped solve a  Rubik's Cube, but it was entirely by accident, and I've been too proud to ever try to complete the puzzle again.
 I wish I could brag and say I mastered the puzzle of the Rubik's Cube through sheer cerebral power. Yet the nine-year-old version of myself who accomplished this feat was barely paying attention at the time. I was spending a weekend afternoon with my friend, Casey, who had been intermittently turning its various faces for several minutes. When he  shouted an unrepeatable expletive, I looked down to see that I had almost solved the damn thing. We studied it together and finished it in a few minutes, then bragged about it to our elementary school chums. They expected us to repeat the task. We never could.
  I'd always wondered what the cube's creator, Ernő Rubik, would think of my experience, and I finally got the chance to ask him. In his new book " Cubed: The Puzzle of Us All," the Hungarian inventor and architect goes into tremendous detail explaining how a bold thirst for knowledge has animated his life. He tells the story of how he invented the toy which bears his name, of course, and how his childhood helped turn him into the man he is today. Going one step beyond this, however, Rubik uses his cube as an allegory for the nature of knowledge itself, a field of study known as epistemology.
 In doing so, Rubik makes a number of assertions, each of them quite wise. He insists that "play," which many  adults dismiss as a childish waste of time, is in fact  essential to both healthy intellectual development and one's capacity to produce great things for society. He argues that curiosity is an underrated virtue in our culture, that we should encourage people to seek knowledge simply because it is fun and cathartic rather than on the condition that the information we find yield some monetary reward. Indeed, although he is clearly not a fan of having grown up "within the economic system of state socialism," Rubik writes that it did bring about the benefit of creating "an overall disregard for financial gain," meaning that creative people could exercise their intellectual powers as fulfilling ends in their own right.
 Perhaps most tellingly, though, Rubik says that he enjoys "the fact that the Cube is a healthy microcosm of both success and failure." For him, of course, it was a success in that it made him "comfortably well-off" before he turned 40. Yet even if it had never become a commercial sensation, Rubik writes he still would have considered it an accomplishment for the simple reason that he was able to invent such a successful puzzle. Beyond that, Rubik notes that even people who fail to successfully solve his puzzle still learn from their efforts to do so.
  The following is a transcript of an email interview with Rubik; as always, this interview has been condensed and edited for print.
 In what ways did your childhood plant the seeds for your ability to develop the Rubik's Cube as an adult?
 As the Cube did not follow from anything that had existed before, it would be very difficult to point to anything in particular. Having said that, I certainly enjoyed playing with brain-teasers from a very young age. I also liked the process of solitary creation, such as drawing and painting. My father was a huge influence, although in comparison, I consider myself to be a creative 'amateur'. My father was both a designer and an inventor and was a professional in his field. In the early 1960's he created his most famous aircraft, the R-26 Góbé, which became the classic training glider in Hungary, which was exported to Cuba, Austria, and the United Kingdom. It certainly occurred to me that every time he figured out the structure and the materials and all the details of his designs, he was solving very practical and complicated puzzles.
   The Cube is very much about understanding the complexity of our world. In some areas, like group theory or combinatorics, this link is very specific and direct. In other realms it is indeed more metaphorical: how to think about problem-solving. What is it about human nature that allows for the luxury of spending so much time and energy figuring out a seemingly impossible challenge, when the reward is nothing more than the solution itself?
  To what extent, when you write this, are you using your relationship with the Rubik's Cube as a metaphor for how a parent feels about a child, or any creator feels about their creation?
 When one becomes a parent, our life changes in very substantial ways. A child becomes a central character in a father's story for the rest of his or her life and beyond. The connection is eternal — but a child has their own, separate life. I very much care for the Cube, I do have a deep sense of responsibility, but I appreciate that the Cube's story is not mine. He became the superstar that I never personally aspired to be.
 The Cube is my creation, but the other parent must be Mother Nature. As much as I created it, it also changed me, and it opened up my life too.
  You admit in your book that you "hate to write," but you did so anyway so that you could gain a better understanding of things that we take for granted, what makes people "tick," what makes people create new things and how they are inspired to make things that have never been created before. To what extent were you able to answer these questions in your book?
 There are no definitive answers to these questions, obviously. My own thinking became clearer on these topics and I hoped I could convey some of those thoughts to the readers of "CUBED." However, the book itself, just like the Cube, never really ends. We can always start again and again revisiting these eternal questions about humanity, and so we should.
 You mention in your book that you are inspired by how the cube has brought people together. What about the cube has made this possible?
  As far as community-building goes, speedcubing has been the most important development. Cubing enthusiasts have gradually created a global sport from purely grassroots effort. It all started in the early 1980's, but really got momentum as the internet provided platforms for niche communities to find each other. Today, there are over 1,000 speedcubing competitions organised every year in over 100 countries. Friendships and even marriages were made thanks to the Cube — but I am afraid the Cube is also to be blamed for a few divorces as well.
 More broadly, what lessons about curiosity do you think people could learn from your invention of the cube? It seems like a message that applies across intellectual, artistic and other professional and creative disciplines.
 I think curiosity is like thirst or hunger. It's a drive that fills a gap, scratches an intellectual and emotional itch. With curiosity, we have the feeling that something is there but we aren't quite sure what it is. It is the flame that can ignite creativity. There is an internal drive to follow up, and a kind of promise that there may be some discovery of something that is hidden and I may be the only one to whom it is not visible. What is under the surface is the need to understand. In that sense, we are not creators but discoverers.
 Puzzles bring out important qualities of ourselves, like curiosity itself among many others. Puzzles are not just entertainment or devices for killing time. For us, as for our ancestors, they help point the way to our creative potential. If you are curious, you will find the puzzles around you. If you are determined, you will solve them.
  Curiosity means that we do not take things for granted, that again and again we question even the fundamentals. We find the good questions because we are interested in the "how" of things. That is the only way forward. And here "forward" doesn't mean a predetermined direction or something that can be fixed beforehand, but the openness, not just of the eye, but of the broader vision.
 You discuss your beliefs about the importance of education while offering critiques about how we do so. Two that stood out to me are when you point out that we don't really prepare young people to develop their potential intelligence and apply that in a meaningful way in the outside world, and your quote that "I have long hated the whole idea of quantifying intelligence with something like an IQ test and sympathize with those who think this test measures only the capacity to perform well on an IQ test." How do you think we should change our approach to education?
 Nowadays what we can really measure in the educational environment is the effort it takes for children to develop their skills.
 The arguable measurement of IQ still has a unique appeal as a solid measure of intelligence, and often we forget about the other crucial aspect of human intelligence: emotion. The capacity to understand human behaviour, to feel, to decode the signs of the people around you, you need to be able to wake up some emotional resonance in them.
  The Cube is one of the very few examples where the so-called edutainment is a seamless experience. The teachers happily accept it as a vehicle to teach and the students enjoy the playful learning the Cube can provide.
 How can people who wish to be autodidacts (which you encourage people to aspire to be) pursue their interests in a way that separates facts from fictions, knowledge from pseudo-knowledge, developing useful skills from engaging in activities that waste their time?
 People don't necessarily "wish" to be autodidacts. They sometimes just enjoy or are curious about something so much that they spend a significant amount of time and effort to understand it properly. True curiosity is always honest and open-ended — it is the very opposite of prejudice or preconception. Just like a puzzle is always a fair thing — and if you still cheat, you're cheating yourself!
 Rubik's book, " Cubed: The Puzzle of Us All," is now on sale from Flatiron Books.
title: ErnőRubik谈为什么他著名的立方体是人类状况的“隐喻”
title_en: Ernő Rubik on why his famous cube is a “metaphor” for the human condition
